TextENGNew: ...When I finished reading the last file and closed the cover, I found myself turning idly toward the window. The eastern sky had gone fully bright, and the red light of morning was filtering in through the gaps between the blinds.
"...Good grief — stayed through till morning again, have I."
I had only meant to skim through it, but found myself reading on and on without stopping.
Occupational hazard, or just a pitiable habit? ...Either way — knowing this is the last day I'll ever be involved, perhaps some part of me simply couldn't let go.
changeReason: Separated two distinct concepts: 職業病 ('occupational hazard') and 貧乏症 ('pitiable habit/stinginess'), which were conflated in the old translation.

TextENGNew: It's truly... a grievous thing, how many lives were lost — and even those who survived had their lives twisted beyond recognition. And I am no exception to that.
changeReason: Old uses 'driven mad' implying insanity, but 人生を大きく狂わされた means lives were greatly disrupted/derailed — a different register of suffering.

TextENGNew: "...Kuma-chan."
The face of the young detective who had worked so tirelessly and eagerly under me floated up behind my eyes, and without thinking I closed them.
changeReason: Corrected the sequence: old version has him close eyes first, but JPN 脳裏に浮かんで、思わず瞑目する has the face floating to mind first, then eyes closing involuntarily.

TextENGNew: On the day of the great disaster, I happened to be summoned to prefectural headquarters on urgent business — so he went to Hinamizawa in my place.
"It'll be fine, Oishi-san! Leave this one to me. You go deal with that mess waiting for you at headquarters."
changeReason: Changed name romanization from 'Ooishi-san' to 'Oishi-san'; reflects a different convention for rendering 大石.

TextENGNew: "You fool..."
Of course, that reproach was not directed at him. It was aimed squarely at myself.
He had gone to Hinamizawa on my behalf, for a case I was pursuing — and it was an investigation that defied orders from above, one that could rightly be called insubordination.
changeReason: Added 'insubordination' (独断) which the old translation omitted; the JPN explicitly characterizes the investigation as potentially insubordinate.

TextENGNew: Being injured or killed in the line of duty is the occupational hazard that comes with this profession, and I had made my peace with that long ago. ...But his case was different.
To put it plainly... he died in my place...
"...... Why am I the one still alive?"
I knew those words were an affront to the dead. And yet I couldn't stop myself from muttering them.
A young man with a family and a future ahead of him has departed this world, while I — who had already staked my life — stand here still, shamefully alive.
changeReason: New version captures 死を賭した自分 ('I who had staked my life') and おめおめと生き恥をさらしている ('shamefully alive'), which the old translation condensed into a less specific phrase.

TextENGNew: Have I survived this long through sheer luck? Or did those lurking in the shadows behind this mystery judge me not even worth killing?
"...You sons of bitches."
changeReason: Changed from singular 'you piece of shit' to plural 'you sons of bitches'; in context, Ooishi is cursing the unknown conspirators (plural).

TextENGNew: The words left my lips before I could stop them — and they were as pointless as spitting at the sky. The truth is, I've learned nothing, and I'm walking away from the front line. The answers remain in the darkness.
changeReason: Old literalizes the idiom 天に唾 (spitting at the sky = futile/self-defeating) as a physical action of looking up at the heavens; new correctly renders it as idiomatic.

TextENGNew: ...I could hear in my mind the mocking laughter of people whose faces I'd never seen, and found my clenched fist tightening of its own accord.
"Haaah..."
For now... this is a case I cannot forget — no, must not forget — but for this one day, I'll tuck it away in a corner of my heart. After all — today is my last day as a detective.
"Well... I suppose I should shower and change clothes, at least to keep up appearances."
It would be rude to stand before my superiors and junior colleagues in a worn, disheveled state as they see me off on my final day.
With that thought, I gathered the weighty, thick stack of files and made my way back to my seat in the criminal investigation division.
Then I took the locker key from my desk drawer and turned toward the shower room.
Just then, the phone on my desk sounded an electronic tone, and the extension indicator light blinked on.
Realizing the call was for my desk, I lifted the receiver and pressed the button.
"Aah, hello?@k Na ha ha ha, good evening — or rather, good morning."
"Ah, Oishi-san?"
changeReason: Changed name romanization from 'Ooishi-san' to 'Oishi-san.'

TextENGNew: The voice on the other end belonged to one of the junior detectives. He should be on night duty today.
He sounded somewhat thrown off by hearing my voice — understandable, really. A veteran detective still at his desk at this hour of the morning is hardly a common sight.
changeReason: Old says 'veteran detectives do head out pretty early' which muddles the observation. Japanese says a veteran being at his desk this early is unusual (異例) — the old phrasing is confusing but not a full reversal.

TextENGNew: "Yes, this is Oishi. Is something the matter?"
changeReason: Changed name romanization from 'Ooishi' to 'Oishi.'

TextENGNew: "Ah, yes. There's a call for you on the outside line. Apparently someone named Akasaka."
"Akasaka? ...Hmm... Oh?"
changeReason: Changed 'Really...?' to 'Hmm... Oh?'; はて……、っ？ expresses gradual recollection, not surprise.

TextENGNew: I tilted my head for a brief moment — then it came back to me at once. Wasn't this the sharp young man from Tokyo who had worked with me on a certain case some time ago?
Good grief — to not recognize someone immediately upon hearing their name... I suppose the years are catching up with me.
"Aah, sorry — that person is an old acquaintance of mine. Could you put him through?"
"Understood."
And shortly after, a familiar voice came through the receiver.
"Oishi-san, it's been a while."
"Oh, Akasaka-san! How long has it been — years, I'd say."
"Five years. You were so very good to me back then."
"Not at all — I wasn't much help to you, I'm afraid."
"That's not true. Thanks to you, I was able to see my mission through — and I learned a great deal beyond that as well. Thank you sincerely."
"Nfu fu fu, enough of that. Keep flattering me like this and even a pig of modest ability will be convinced it can climb trees."
changeReason: New version preserves the Japanese idiom おだてられた豚が木に登る ('a flattered pig climbs a tree'), which the old translation replaced with a generic statement about ability.

TextENGNew: I deflected it with a laugh — but having spent so long in a trade surrounded by human lies and deception, his earnestness, audible even through the phone, was genuinely warming.
changeReason: New version adds 'audible even through the phone' (言葉からも伝わる), capturing that the sincerity comes through in his words, which the old translation omitted.

TextENGNew: A truly fine man. If someone like him steadily climbs the ranks and takes on a position of real influence, the future of the police is bright indeed.
changeReason: New version adds the institutional perspective (警察の前途も洋々 = 'the future of the police is bright') which the old translation omitted in favor of focusing only on the individual.

TextENGNew: "So then, Akasaka-san. What brings you to call today?"
"Of course — I'm calling to offer my congratulations. Thank you for your forty years of dedicated service."
"Ah... how thoughtful, really. You're very well-informed — who did you hear it from?"
"From Honda-ya-san in your public security division. He told me during joint training last week."
"Aah, I see, I see. That's how it happened."
"...I heard a number of things as well. I'm sorry it took me until today to reach out."
changeReason: Changed 'it came up when we were sharing stories' to 'I heard a number of things'; the JPN 色々とお話をお伺いしました is a polite 'I heard various things,' not casual chat.

TextENGNew: "...... I'm touched by your consideration."
changeReason: Old translates 痛み入ります as 'embarrassing me' (discomfort) when it means 'I'm deeply grateful/touched' — different emotional register.

TextENGNew: ...Honda-ya had likely told him about the Hinamizawa affair — and about my subordinate's death in the line of duty.
Of course, there was no reason for Akasaka-san to apologize, nor any cause for me to reproach him. Not in the slightest.
And yet... learning of it must have filled him with regret. If only he had known sooner — perhaps he could have joined the investigation, found some piece of information, been of use to me...
And I, too, carry one more regret.
In the immediate aftermath of the disaster, I had been terrified of drawing anyone else in. I could not bear to create another victim like Kumagai.
...But as a result, the investigation into Hinamizawa stalled completely, and will most likely die with my retirement.
To face such an incomprehensible, ruthless... and cunning opponent alone and still accomplish nothing — I should have gathered allies. I should have.
changeReason: Changed 'horrible' to 'ruthless... and cunning'; 辛辣で狡猾な ('harsh and cunning') specifically describes the opponent's craftiness, which 'horrible' does not capture.

TextENGNew: "That is why, Oishi-san — I intend to stop by today. I apologize for the imposition, coming as an outsider."
changeReason: Old renders お邪魔させていただきます as 'had to bother you today' (suggesting a call), while JPN means 'I will come visit in person' — the mode of interaction differs.

TextENGNew: "Oh, not at all... Wait — Akasaka-san, you're coming all the way from Tokyo? Please, don't trouble yourself. All this fuss just for an old man like me... na ha ha ha!"
changeReason: Old says 'calling me all the way out from Tokyo' implying Ooishi was summoned; JPN わざわざ東京から means Akasaka is the one traveling from Tokyo — wrong subject of the action.

TextENGNew: "What are you saying? This much doesn't even begin to repay the debt I owe you. I hope you'll continue to guide and encourage me, and I ask for a long acquaintance going forward."
changeReason: Old reverses the direction of ご鞭撻いただきたい: Akasaka is humbly requesting Ooishi's continued guidance, but old says Akasaka wants to encourage Ooishi.

TextENGNew: "...Akasaka-san."
What a fine young man... no, I should stop being so condescending.
changeReason: Clarified that Ooishi realizes calling Akasaka a 'young man' is condescending; the old translation vaguely referred to past disrespect.

TextENGNew: And yet — to make the long journey here in the midst of such demanding work. I could think of nothing more heartening. Kindness unearned, they say — but I was truly proud to have known him.
changeReason: Old mistranslates proverb 情けは人のためならず (kindness comes back around) as 'people like that get little sympathy' — opposite sentiment.

TextENGNew: That's it.
Perhaps he... could do it, where I could not.
The truth that lies on the other side of this mystery — the one I could never reach on my own...
"Akasaka-san — if you don't mind, I was hoping to bend your ear on a matter this evening?"
He may find it a burden. Even so, I reached out — clinging to that single thread of hope.
